distribution_analysis.most_covered_section: 4.2 Minors and Cofactors of Elements of Matrices

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: For a \(2 \times 2\) matrix \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}\), the determinant is defined as \(\det(\mathbf{A}) = ad - bc\). What is the value of \(\det\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}\)?
questions.hint: Multiply the main diagonal entries and subtract the product of the off-diagonal entries.
questions.explanation: The determinant formula \(ad - bc\) gives \(5 \times 4 - 2 \times 3 = 20 - 6 = 14\). Option D results from \(bc - ad\), the negative of the correct determinant.

explanations_tiered.definition: The determinant of a \(2 \times 2\) matrix \(\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}\) is the scalar \(ad - bc\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The determinant is a single number derived from the entries of a square matrix. For a 2x2 matrix, it measures the signed area scaling factor of the linear transformation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of the matrix as a box with four numbers. Multiply the top-left and bottom-right, then subtract top-right times bottom-left.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The determinant formula \(ad - bc\) combines all four entries. For \(\begin{pmatrix}5&2\\3&4\end{pmatrix}\), compute \(5\times4 - 2\times3 = 20-6=14\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The determinant of a 2x2 matrix is the signed area of the parallelogram formed by its column vectors. For \(\begin{pmatrix}5&2\\3&4\end{pmatrix}\), the area is \(|5\cdot4 - 2\cdot3| = 14\) square units.
reasoning_steps: Identify the values of \(a, b, c, d\) from the matrix.
Subtract the second product from the first.
misconceptions: \(bc - ad\) is incorrect because the determinant is defined as \(ad - bc\), not the reverse.
Some mistakenly add all products: \(ad + bc\) yields 26, but the correct operation is subtraction, not addition.

glossary_entry.definition: A scalar value computed from the entries of a square matrix that encodes properties such as invertibility and volume scaling.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A scalar computed from square matrix entries that indicates invertibility and scaling properties.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: students compute \(bc - ad\) instead. Emphasize the order of subtraction.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Using \(ad - bc = 5\times4 - 2\times3 = 20-6=14\). Option D is the negative.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: For what value(s) of \(x\) does the matrix \(\begin{pmatrix} 1 & x \\ 2 & x+1 \end{pmatrix}\) have a determinant equal to zero?
questions.hint: Set \(ad - bc = 0\) and solve the linear equation for \(x\).
questions.explanation: Setting \(\det = 1(x+1) - x(2) = x+1-2x = 1 - x = 0\) gives \(x = 1\). Option B would satisfy \(1 - (-1) = 2 \neq 0\), which is a common sign error.

explanations_tiered.definition: A matrix is singular (non-invertible) when its determinant equals zero.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Setting the determinant to zero creates an equation in \(x\). Solving that equation finds values where the matrix loses invertibility.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Write the determinant as \(1\times(x+1) - x\times2\). Set it equal to zero and solve for \(x\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The equation \(1-x=0\) is linear with unique solution \(x=1\). At this value, the rows become linearly dependent, so the determinant is zero.
worked_solution: Step 4: Set equal to zero: \(1-x=0 \Rightarrow x=1\).
misconceptions: Some incorrectly compute \(1\cdot x - (x+1)\cdot2\) giving a different expression.
Others forget to set the determinant to zero and instead solve \(1-x = \text{something else}\).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Set \(ad-bc=0\), solve for variable. Here: \(1(x+1)-2x=0 \Rightarrow x=1\).

glossary_entry.definition: A square matrix whose determinant is zero and which therefore does not have an inverse.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A matrix whose determinant is zero, making it non-invertible.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often mishandle the sign when expanding \(1(x+1)-2x\). Drill the order \(ad - bc\).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine unknown entries that make a matrix singular
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. \(\det = 1(x+1)-2x = 1-x = 0 \Rightarrow x=1\). Option B gives \(1-(-1)=2 \neq 0\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 4.2 Minors and Cofactors of Elements of Matrices
questions.question: Given a \(3 \times 3\) matrix \(\mathbf{A}\), the minor \(M_{23}\) is defined as the determinant of the matrix obtained by deleting which row and column?
questions.hint: The subscript \(ij\) in \(M_{ij}\) indicates the row \(i\) and column \(j\) to remove.
questions.explanation: By definition, \(M_{ij}\) is the determinant after deleting the \(i\)-th row and \(j\)-th column. Option A correctly identifies row 2 and column 3. Option B reverses the indices, a common confusion.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.2 Minors and Cofactors of Elements of Matrices
source_trace.content_anchor: cross out the ith row and jth column

explanations_tiered.definition: The minor \(M_{ij}\) of a matrix \(\mathbf{A}\) is the determinant of the submatrix formed by deleting the \(i\)-th row and \(j\)-th column.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Minors are building blocks for cofactors and determinants. The notation \(M_{ij}\) explicitly tells which row and column to remove.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The two numbers in the subscript tell you which row and which column to cross out before finding the determinant.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For \(M_{23}\), delete row 2 and column 3 from the original matrix, then compute the determinant of the remaining \(2 \times 2\) submatrix.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Minors are essential for Laplace expansion. The index order is always (row, column), so \(M_{23}\) removes the second row and third column.
reasoning_steps: Delete that specific row and column from the matrix.
Compute the determinant of the remaining smaller matrix.
misconceptions: Some students reverse the order, thinking \(M_{23}\) means delete row 3 and column 2.
Others confuse minor with cofactor, forgetting the sign factor \((-1)^{i+j}\).

glossary_entry.definition: The determinant of a square submatrix obtained by deleting one row and one column from a larger square matrix.

flashcard.front: What is deleted to obtain minor \(M_{23}\)?
flashcard.back: Row 2 and column 3 are deleted from the original matrix before taking the determinant.

concept_map_nodes.target: Deleting row i and column j

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The minor obtained by deleting row 2 and column 3, then taking the determinant.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The determinant of the submatrix formed by deleting one row and one column.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: \(M_{ij}\) = delete row i, column j, then take det.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that the order is always (row, column) to avoid index reversal errors.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define minor of a matrix entry

concept_map_nodes.target: Deletion of row i and column j

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The subscript \(ij\) indicates row i and column j. \(M_{23}\) uses row 2, column 3.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 4.2 Minors and Cofactors of Elements of Matrices
questions.question: For matrix \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 3 & -1 & 5 \end{pmatrix}\), what is the minor \(M_{11}\)?
questions.hint: Delete row 1 and column 1, then compute the determinant of the remaining \(2 \times 2\) matrix.
questions.explanation: Deleting row 1 and column 1 leaves \(\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\-1&5\end{pmatrix}\) with determinant \(1\times5 - 0\times(-1)=5\). Option C is \(M_{13}\), a common confusion of column indices.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.2 Minors and Cofactors of Elements of Matrices

explanations_tiered.definition: Minor \(M_{11}\) is the determinant of the submatrix after removing the first row and first column.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Minors are computed by elimination. For \(M_{11}\), the resulting 2x2 matrix's determinant is calculated using the standard formula.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Cross out the first row and first column. The four numbers left form a small box; multiply its diagonal and subtract.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: After deleting row1 and col1, we get \(\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\-1&5\end{pmatrix}\). Its determinant is \(1\times5 - 0\times(-1)=5\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The minor \(M_{11}\) is independent of the first row and column entries. Here the remaining submatrix is triangular, so its determinant is simply the product of diagonal entries: \(1\times5=5\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Remove row 1 and column 1 from A.
reasoning_steps: Identify which row and column to delete (i=1, j=1).
misconceptions: Some students delete the wrong row/column (e.g., row1 col2 for M11).
Others incorrectly compute the determinant as \(1\times(-1) - 0\times5 = -1\), mixing up positions.

glossary_entry.definition: The determinant of the submatrix obtained by removing the first row and first column.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The determinant of a submatrix formed by deleting one row and one column.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Have students practice deleting different rows/columns to reinforce the definition.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. \(M_{11}= \det\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\-1&5\end{pmatrix}=5\). Option C is M13.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 4.2 Minors and Cofactors of Elements of Matrices
questions.question: The cofactor \(C_{ij}\) of a matrix entry \(a_{ij}\) is defined as \(C_{ij} = (-1)^{i+j} M_{ij}\). For \(i=2, j=3\), the sign factor \((-1)^{i+j}\) equals:
questions.hint: Compute \((-1)^{2+3} = (-1)^5\). An odd exponent gives negative.
questions.explanation: \(2+3=5\), which is odd, so \((-1)^5 = -1\). Option A would be correct for an even sum (e.g., \(i=j\)). This sign alternates like a chessboard pattern.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.2 Minors and Cofactors of Elements of Matrices

explanations_tiered.definition: The cofactor \(C_{ij}\) is the signed minor: \(C_{ij} = (-1)^{i+j} M_{ij}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The sign factor \((-1)^{i+j}\) alternates like a checkerboard: positive when \(i+j\) is even, negative when odd.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Add the row and column numbers. If the sum is even, the cofactor has a plus sign; if odd, a minus sign.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The sign pattern ensures consistency in determinant expansion and adjoint matrix construction. \((-1)^{i+j}\) is +1 on the main diagonal and alternates off-diagonally.
worked_solution: Step 1: Add the row index and column index: \(2+3=5\).
reasoning_steps: Check if the sum is even or odd.
If even, sign = +1; if odd, sign = -1.
misconceptions: Some think \((-1)^{i+j}\) means \(-1\) multiplied by \(i+j\), which is incorrect.
Others forget that \((-1)^{\text{even}}=+1\) and \((-1)^{\text{odd}}=-1\).

glossary_entry.definition: The signed minor of a matrix entry, used in determinant expansion and inverse calculation.

flashcard.front: What is the sign factor for cofactor \(C_{23}\)?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw a 3x3 checkerboard with + and - signs to visualize the alternating pattern.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \((-1)^{2+3}=(-1)^5=-1\). Option A would be for even sum like \(1+1=2\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Using cofactor expansion along the first row, the determinant of \(\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{pmatrix}\) equals \(1C_{11} + 2C_{12} + 3C_{13}\). What is the value of \(C_{11}\)?
questions.hint: \(C_{11} = (-1)^{1+1} M_{11}\). Delete row1 and col1, then compute the 2x2 determinant.
questions.explanation: \(M_{11} = \det\begin{pmatrix}5&6\\8&9\end{pmatrix} = 45-48 = -3\). Since \((-1)^{2}=+1\), \(C_{11} = -3\). Option D (\(-6\)) is \(C_{12}\) or a sign error on the 2x2 determinant.

explanations_tiered.definition: Cofactor \(C_{11}\) is the signed minor for the (1,1) entry: \(C_{11}=(-1)^{1+1}M_{11}=M_{11}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For the first row, first column, the sign is positive. \(M_{11}\) is the determinant after removing row1 and col1.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Cover the first row and first column. The four numbers left form a small box; find its determinant (diagonal minus other diagonal).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Removing row1 and col1 leaves \(\begin{pmatrix}5&6\\8&9\end{pmatrix}\). Its determinant is \(5\times9 - 6\times8 = 45-48 = -3\). The sign is positive because \(1+1=2\) is even.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The cofactor expansion formula requires all three cofactors. \(C_{11}\) alone is \(-3\). The other cofactors are \(C_{12}=6\) and \(C_{13}=-3\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify i=1, j=1. Remove row1 and col1.
reasoning_steps: Delete the row and column of the target entry.
Compute the determinant of the resulting smaller matrix.
misconceptions: Some forget the sign factor, but here it's positive anyway.
Others compute \(5\times8 - 6\times9 = 40-54=-14\), mixing up row/column order in the 2x2 determinant.

glossary_entry.definition: The signed minor for the (1,1) entry; here \((-1)^{2}M_{11}=M_{11}=-3\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is \(C_{11}\) for the 3x3 matrix with rows [1,2,3], [4,5,6], [7,8,9]?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Signed minor used in determinant expansion.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Point out that the 3x3 determinant of this matrix is zero, but individual cofactors are nonzero.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: Cofactor Expansion of Determinant along Any Row
questions.structural_anchor: Cofactor Expansion of Determinant along Any Row
questions.question: For matrix \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 7 & 2 \\ 0 & 5 & 2 \\ -1 & 4 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\), expanding along the second row gives \(\det(\mathbf{A}) = a_{21}C_{21} + a_{22}C_{22} + a_{23}C_{23}\). Given that \(C_{21} = -\det\begin{pmatrix}7&2\\4&1\end{pmatrix}\), what is the value of \(C_{21}\)?
questions.hint: Compute \(\det\begin{pmatrix}7&2\\4&1\end{pmatrix} = 7\times1 - 2\times4 = 7-8 = -1\). Then apply the negative sign.
questions.explanation: \(\det\begin{pmatrix}7&2\\4&1\end{pmatrix} = 7(1) - 2(4) = 7-8 = -1\). Then \(C_{21} = -(-1) = 1\). Option B would be the minor itself without the cofactor sign.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Cofactor Expansion of Determinant along Any Row
source_trace.structural_element: Cofactor Expansion of Determinant along Any Row

explanations_tiered.definition: Cofactor \(C_{21}\) = \((-1)^{2+1} M_{21} = -M_{21}\), where \(M_{21}\) is the minor after deleting row2 and col1.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The sign for \(i=2,j=1\) is negative because \(2+1=3\) is odd. Compute the minor first, then apply the sign.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Delete row 2 and column 1. Find the determinant of the remaining 2x2 box. Then make it negative because 2+1 is odd.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Cofactor expansion often uses rows with zeros. Here row2 has a zero at \(a_{21}=0\), so \(C_{21}\) is not needed in the final sum, but computing it shows the pattern.
worked_solution: Step 1: Delete row2 and col1 → submatrix \(\begin{pmatrix}7&2\\4&1\end{pmatrix}\).
reasoning_steps: Multiply by \((-1)^{2+1}\) to get the cofactor.
misconceptions: Some forget the negative sign for odd \(i+j\) and give \(C_{21}=-1\) instead of +1.
Others compute the 2x2 determinant incorrectly as \(7\times4 - 2\times1 = 28-2=26\), misaligning rows and columns.

glossary_entry.definition: The signed minor for the (2,1) entry; here \(C_{21}=1\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Compute \(C_{21}\) for the matrix with rows [1,7,2], [0,5,2], [-1,4,1].
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that \((-1)^{\text{odd}}\) gives a negative sign outside the minor.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. \(M_{21}=7\cdot1-2\cdot4=-1\), \(C_{21}=(-1)^{3}(-1)=1\). Option B is \(M_{21}\) without sign.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: For an upper triangular matrix \(\mathbf{U} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & -1 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\), the determinant equals:
questions.hint: The determinant of a triangular matrix is the product of its diagonal entries.
questions.explanation: For an upper triangular matrix, \(\det = 2 \times (-1) \times 1 = -2\). Option A is the product with sign error (ignoring the negative). Triangular matrices are common in row reduction.

explanations_tiered.definition: The determinant of a triangular matrix (upper or lower) is the product of its diagonal entries.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Triangular matrices have zeros either above or below the main diagonal. Their determinant simplifies dramatically to just the diagonal product.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Multiply the numbers on the diagonal going from top-left to bottom-right. Ignore all other numbers.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For this upper triangular matrix, diagonal entries are 2, -1, and 1. Product = \(2 \times (-1) \times 1 = -2\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: This property follows from cofactor expansion repeated along the first column (for lower triangular) or first row (for upper triangular), which leaves only the diagonal product.
reasoning_steps: Check that the matrix is triangular (all zeros below main diagonal for upper triangular).
misconceptions: Some students include off-diagonal entries in the product.
Others forget that the determinant can be negative and give +2 instead of -2.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Triangular matrix det = product of diagonal entries. Example: \(2\times(-1)\times1=-2\).

glossary_entry.definition: A square matrix with zeros either above (lower triangular) or below (upper triangular) the main diagonal.

flashcard.back: \(-2\). For an upper triangular matrix, multiply diagonals: \(2 \times (-1) \times 1 = -2\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you find the determinant of a triangular matrix?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Matrix with zeros either above or below the main diagonal; its determinant is the diagonal product.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: This is a powerful shortcut. Show why it works by expanding along the first column for lower triangular matrices.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Upper triangular → product of diagonal: \(2 \times (-1) \times 1 = -2\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If two rows of a square matrix are interchanged, what happens to the determinant?
questions.hint: Recall the effect of swapping rows on determinant from elementary row operations.
questions.explanation: Interchanging two rows multiplies the determinant by \(-1\), i.e., it changes sign. Option A would occur if rows were identical, not just swapped. Option D is true for adding a multiple of one row to another.

source_trace.content_anchor: Interchanging two rows changes the sign of the determinant

explanations_tiered.definition: Row interchange is an elementary row operation that multiplies the determinant by \(-1\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Swapping two rows reverses the orientation of the matrix's row vectors, which changes the sign of the signed volume (determinant).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine swapping two rows of a 2x2 matrix: \(\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}\) becomes \(\begin{pmatrix}c&d\\a&b\end{pmatrix}\). The determinant goes from \(ad-bc\) to \(cb-da = -(ad-bc)\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For any square matrix, swapping any two rows changes the determinant's sign. This is true regardless of matrix size.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This property follows from the alternating nature of the determinant as a multilinear form. Swapping two arguments introduces a factor of \(-1\).
reasoning_steps: Perform a single row swap operation.
misconceptions: Some think swapping rows makes the determinant zero (true only if rows are identical).
Others confuse this with adding a multiple of one row to another (which leaves determinant unchanged).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Row swap → determinant changes sign (multiply by \(-1\)).

glossary_entry.definition: An elementary row operation that exchanges two rows of a matrix, changing the sign of the determinant.

flashcard.front: What is the effect of swapping two rows on a determinant?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Swapping two rows of a matrix changes the determinant how?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Operation that exchanges two rows; determinant is multiplied by \(-1\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with a 2x2 example: \(\det\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}=ad-bc\). After swap: \(cb-da = -(ad-bc)\).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe effects of row operations on determinants
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Swapping two rows changes the sign. Option A is for equal rows; D is for adding a multiple of one row to another.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: For two \(3 \times 3\) matrices \(\mathbf{A}\) and \(\mathbf{B}\) with \(\det(\mathbf{A}) = 4\) and \(\det(\mathbf{B}) = 5\), what is \(\det(3\mathbf{A})\)?
questions.hint: For an \(n \times n\) matrix, \(\det(k\mathbf{A}) = k^n \det(\mathbf{A})\). Here \(n=3\).
questions.explanation: \(\det(3\mathbf{A}) = 3^3 \det(\mathbf{A}) = 27 \times 4 = 108\). Option A is \(3 \times 4\) forgetting the exponent. Option B is \(4 \times 5\), the product of determinants of A and B.

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Multiplying every entry of a matrix by \(k\) scales each row by \(k\), so the determinant scales by \(k\) per row, giving \(k^n\) total.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When you multiply the whole matrix by 3, each of the 3 rows gets multiplied by 3. So the determinant is multiplied by 3 three times, or \(3^3=27\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: This property follows from the multilinearity of the determinant: factoring a scalar out of each of the \(n\) rows contributes a factor of \(k^n\).
reasoning_steps: Identify the size of the matrix (n=3).
Raise the scalar to the nth power: \(3^3 = 27\).
misconceptions: Many students incorrectly use \(\det(k\mathbf{A}) = k \det(\mathbf{A})\), forgetting the exponent \(n\).
Others confuse this with \(\det(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B}) = \det(\mathbf{A})\det(\mathbf{B})\) and multiply 4 by 5 to get 20 (not among options but a distractor).

glossary_entry.definition: Multiplying every entry of a matrix by a scalar \(k\); the determinant scales by \(k^n\).

flashcard.front: If \(\det(\mathbf{A})=4\) for a 3x3 matrix, what is \(\det(3\mathbf{A})\)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: For a 3x3 matrix A with det(A)=4, find det(3A).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The determinant of a scalar times a matrix equals the scalar raised to the matrix order times the original determinant.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize the exponent: each row gets multiplied, so n factors of k.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply determinant properties to scalar multiplication
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. For an n×n matrix, \(\det(kA)=k^n\det(A)\). Here n=3, k=3, so \(3^3×4=108\). Option A is \(3×4\), forgetting the exponent.
